quotes.text: Apparently Corianton followed the counsel of his father, repented of his sins, went on his mission, and remained faithful to the Lord's teachings. At the end of the book of Alma we read: "And [Shiblon] was a just man, and he did walk uprightly before God; and he did observe to do good continually, to keep the commandments of the Lord his God; and also did his brother." (Alma 63:2.) Since Shiblon's older brother, Helaman, had already died (Alma 62:52), it seems likely that the righteous brother described in this verse is Corianton (also see Alma 49:30; 63:10).
quotes.speaker: Brent A. Barlow
quotes.source: Worth Waiting For: Sexual Abstinence Before Marriage [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co.
quotes.attr: Brent A. Barlow, Worth Waiting For: Sexual Abstinence Before Marriage [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1995], 113

quotes.text: Moroni died also in the same year. The next year, which was 56 B.C., the valiant Moroni, one of the greatest and most virtuous of God's sons, passed away from this state of mortality to the glories of eternity, at the early age of forty-three years. Some time before his death he had given the chief command of the armies of the Nephites to his son, Moronihah, who from the history of later years, we judge him to have been a worthy son of so illustrious a sire.
quotes.speaker: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl
quotes.source: Commentary on the Book of Mormon
quotes.attr: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl, Commentary on the Book of Mormon, edited and arranged by Philip C. Reynolds, 7 vols., 5:, p.184

notes: In the Church it is generally held that Hagoth was the father of the Polyesians, that his expeditions to the isles of the sea were a part of the foreordained plan whereby the descendants of father Lehi, as children of Abraham, might be spread to all nations and thus fulfill God's covenant with the father of the faithful (Abraham 2:8-11). In speaking to the Saints in Samoa, President Spencer W. Kimball said: "I thought to read to you a sacred scripture which pertains especially to you, the islanders of the Pacific. It is in the sixty-third chapter of Alma. [He reads Alma 63:4,7-10.] And so it seems to me rather clear that your ancestors moved northward and crossed a part of the South Pacific. You did not bring your records with you, but you brought much food and provisions. And so we have a great congregation of people in the South Seas who came from the Nephites, and who came from the land southward and went to the land northward, which could have been Hawaii. And then the further settlement could have been a move southward again to all of these islands and even to New Zealand. The Lord knows what he is doing when he sends his people from one place to another. That was the scattering of Israel. Some of them remained in America and went from Alaska to the southern point. And others of you came this direction." (Samoa Area Conference Report, February 1976, p. 15.) To another group of Saints in the South Seas, President Kimball observed: "President Joseph F. Smith, the president of the Church, reported, 'You brothers and sisters from New Zealand, I want you to know that you are from the people of Hagoth.' For New Zealand Saints, that was that. A prophet of the Lord had spoken.... It is reasonable to conclude that Hagoth and his associates were about nineteen centuries on the islands, from about 55 B.C. to 1854 before the gospel began to reach them. They had lost all the plain and precious things which the Savior brought to the earth, for they were likely on the islands when the Christ was born in Jerusalem." (Temple View Area Conference Report, February 1976, p. 3.) Elder Matthew Cowley: "When I was on my first mission as a young boy, I used to ask the oldtimers out there, 'Where did you come from' They would say (in Maori), 'We came from the place where the sweet potato grows wild, where it is not planted, and does not have to be cultivated.' "There is only one place in all the world where the sweet potato grows wild, and that is within the environs of that narrow neck of land where Hagoth built his ships. They will tell you that they came from several degrees of distance (Maori). One degree of distance, a greater degree of distance, and then a far greater degree of distance. "The Maori scholars tell you that means the place where the spirits are joined. But I have a little different interpretation of that. Wairua in the Maori language means 'spirit.' Wairua also means 'two waters, 'wai meaning water, rua meaning two. "In the Hawaiian language Wailua means 'two waters'; in the Samoan language Vailua means 'two waters.' The word for spirit in those other languages isn't Waima, the same as in the Maori language. "The Maori scholars say that they came from the far distant place, where the spirits are joined, or where the body returns to the spirit. But I say, knowing the story of Hagoth as I do, that they came from the joining of two waters, a narrow neck of land between two bodies of water which joins those two great

"When I was on my first mission as a young boy, I used to ask the old-timers out there, 'Where did you come from?' They would say (in Maori), 'We came from the place where the sweet potato grows wild, where it is not planted, does not have to be cultivated.' "There is only one place in all the world where the sweet potato grows wild, and that is within the environs of that narrow neck of land where Hagoth built his ships. They will tell you that they came from several degrees of distance (Maori). One degree of distance, a greater degree of distance, and then a far greater degree of distance.

quotes.text: Speaking of Hagoth and his party, President Spencer W. Kimball said: "President Joseph F. Smith, the president of the Church reported, 'You brethren and sisters from New Zealand, I want you to know that you are from the people of Hagoth.' For New Zealand Saints, that was that. A prophet of the Lord had spoken
quotes.speaker: New Zealand Area Conference Report
quotes.attr: in New Zealand Area Conference Report, 20–22 Feb. 1976, p. 3

quotes.text: Hagoth -A Nephite ship-builder and promoter of emigration. He was a very ingenious mechanic and in the thirty-seventh year of the Judges (B.C. 55) he settled on the Pacific side of the Isthmus of Panama, where the lands Desolation and Bountiful ran parallel. There he built an exceedingly large ship and launched it on the Pacific Ocean. This ship he filled with men, women and children, after which it set sail northward; and having delivered its living freight it returned the next year, again to start northward loaded with passengers and provisions. He also built other ships which engaged in the same trade. Some of these never reached their destination; they were either lost in the depths of the sea or were carried by storms and adverse winds to some of the many groups of islands that dot the Pacific Ocean. In this manner it is more than probable the Hawaiian Islands were peopled with the ancestors of the present inhabitants. The loss of these vessels did not stop the outflow by sea northward. The voyage obviated the tedious land journey through the regions now known as Central America and Mexico. At what point these emigrants disembarked is solely a matter of conjecture and it is highly probable that the configuration of the western coast, northward from the Isthmus, was very different, previous to the immense convulsions and upheavals that occurred at the death of Christ, to what it is today. From this time the Nephites paid considerable attention to ship-building, and the sea became the highway between the two western continents.
quotes.speaker: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl
quotes.source: Commentary on the Book of Mormon
quotes.attr: George Reynolds and Janne M. Sjodahl, Commentary on the Book of Mormon, edited and arranged by Philip C. Reynolds, 7 vols., 5:, p.184-185

quotes.text: What about the LDS tradition that Hagoth, the Nephite shipbuilder who failed to return home, was an ancestor of the Polynesians? Years ago I compiled a large body of shared culture traits that indeed suggest historical links between those islands and various parts of the Americas, and this has been supplemented by others. Yet the evidence does not allow our pinning down any single time or place for a migration of trade that would persuasively explain the similarities. It remains impossible to demonstrate any clearcut connection between the two areas, although debate continues. Having been a missionary in Polynesia, I am well aware of the Hagoth theme in LDS tradition, but the evidence available does not support it as historically based fact. Neither can we rule out the possibility of a rare voyage between the mainland and the islands. Most of the evidence cited one way or the other is either weak or unclear. Those who choose to believe that Hagoth reached Polynesia must rely mainly on faith rather than on reliable evidence. The Book of Mormon itself, of course, says only that the man and his mates disappeared from the knowledge of the people in Zarahemla. For all they knew he might have died at a ripe old age on the west Mexican coast without a suitable vessel in which to make the return voyage. And neither do we know.
quotes.speaker: John L. Sorenson
quotes.source: An Ancient American Setting for the Book of Mormon [Salt Lake City and Provo: Deseret Book Co.
quotes.attr: John L. Sorenson, An Ancient American Setting for the Book of Mormon [Salt Lake City and Provo: Deseret Book Co., Foundation for Ancient Research and Mormon Studies, 1985], 269

quotes.text: Hagoth built large ships and sailed north. His destination was never known by the Nephites. Latter-day prophets have declared that Hagoth's ships reached the islands of the Pacific, where they became part of the Polynesian culture. In 1913, responding to a telegram that he had received earlier that "perhaps" they were some of Hagoth's people, President Joseph F. Smith told a group of Polynesian Saints: "I would like to say to you brethren and sisters from New Zealand, you are some of Hagoth's people, and there is NO PERHAPS about it!" He explained that when he was a missionary in Hawaii the Spirit had testified to him that the Polynesians were descended from Lehi (in William A. Cole and Elwin W. Jensen, Israel in the Pacific [1961], 388). Later, in the dedicatory prayer of the Hawaii temple, President Heber J. Grant thanked Heavenly Father that "thousands and tens of thousands of the descendants of Lehi, in this favored land, have come to a knowledge of the gospel" ("The Dedicatory Prayer in the Hawaiian Temple," Improvement Era, Feb. 1920, 283).
quotes.speaker: Book of Mormon Student Study Guide
quotes.attr: Book of Mormon Student Study Guide, p. 143

quotes.text: The belief that Polynesian ancestry includes Book of Mormon people can be traced back at least to 1851, when George Q. Cannon taught it as a missionary in Hawaii (he was later a counselor in the First Presidency). President Brigham Young detailed the belief in a letter to King Kamehameha V in 1865. Other Church leaders have since affirmed the belief, some indicating that among Polynesian ancestors were the people of Hagoth, who set sail from Nephite lands in approximately 54 B.C. (cf. Alma 63:5-8). In a statement to the Maoris of New Zealand, for instance, President Joseph F. Smith said, "I would like to say to you brethren and sisters . . . you are some of Hagoth's people, and there is NO PERHAPS about it!" (Cole and Jensen, p. 388.) In the prayer offered at the dedication of the Hawaii Temple, President Heber J. Grant referred to the "descendants of Lehi" in Hawaii (IE 23 [Feb. 1920]:283).
quotes.speaker: Encyclopedia of Mormonism

quotes.text: Members of New Zealand's indigenous community are claiming to be descendants of the 10 lost tribes of Israel, the vacation journal Tourist Guide reported last weekend. A first group of several dozen Maoris, as the country's native population is known, is expected to arrive here soon, in order to renew their ties with the Jewish people. According to the article, the Maori believe they are descendants of the tribe of Ephraim, who made their way to the South Pacific islands 3,000 years ago, and see a certain similarity between their religion and Judaism. Yoram Hirschler, representative of the Air New Zealand airline said that the Israeli Ambassador, Lydia Shukrum, told him that the first group of 80-100 Maoris who feel themselves to be Jews will arrive for a visit during the millennium year.
quotes.attr: Yair Sheleg and Irit Rosenblum Ha'aretz Newspaper, Novemeber 28, 1999

notes: This is one of the subtle testimonies of the truthfulness of this record. Had Joseph Smith simply been creating the Book of Mormon, fabricating it (rather than translating it), he probably would not have inserted such ideas into the narrative. Here we see that Mormon, a powerful prophet-editor, was simply unaware of what became of Hagoth and his followers. Living almost five centuries after their departure from the promised land, Mormon could have inquired as to their whereabouts, but presumably he had

quotes.text: When I was on my first mission as a young boy, I used to ask the oldtimers out there, 'Where did you come from' They would say (in Maori), 'We came from the place where the sweet potato grows wild, where it is not planted, and does not have to be cultivated.' "There is only one place in all the world where the sweet potato grows wild, and that is within the environs of that narrow neck of land where Hagoth built his ships. They will tell you that they came from several degrees of distance (Maori). One degree of distance, a greater degree of distance, and then a far greater degree of distance. "The Maori scholars tell you that means the place where the spirits are joined. But I have a little different interpretation of that. Wairua in the Maori language means 'spirit.' Wairua also means 'two waters, 'wai meaning water, rua meaning two. "In the Hawaiian language Wailua means 'two waters'; in the Samoan language Vailua means 'two waters.' The word for spirit in those other languages isn't Waima, the same as in the Maori language."The Maori scholars say that they came from the far distant place, where the spirits arejoined, or where the body returns to the spirit. But I say, knowing the story of Hagoth as I do,that they came from the joining of two waters, a narrow neck of land between two bodies ofwater which joins those two great continents.
quotes.speaker: Matthew Cowley
quotes.attr: Matthew Cowley, Matthew Cowley Speaks, pp.114-15

notes: Moronihah - A great Nephite general and prophet, of the days of the Republic. He succeeded his father, Moroni, in the command of the Nephite armies 60 B.C. and the fact of his father resigning in his favor is the first mention made of him in the Book of Mormon. He must have been, at that time, still a very young man as his father was only about 39 years old but he had undoubtedly been trained from his boyhood in military affairs in the great war of seven years' duration against Amalickiah and Ammoron which had only been brought to an end the year previous. It was not until 53 B.C. that his armies were called upon to take the field. In that year the Lamanites, urged by the Nephite apostates within their borders, invaded the land of Zarahemla but Moronihah met and defeated them and they were driven back to their own dominions with great loss. In 51 B.C. the Lamanites again invaded Zarahemla. They were led by an officer named Coriantumr. He adopted new tactics. Moronihah, believing that the Lamanites dared not push through the centre of the land but would, as was their custom first attack outlying cities, placed his strongest garrisons in those places. Coriantumr, however, made a dash directly towards the Nephite capital through the densest of the Nephite population and carried everything before him. The Nephites surprised and unprepared as they were, resisted him only in small bodies which were easily dispersed and he reached and captured the City of Zarahemla before Moronihah could concentrate his forces. The whole country fell into Coriantumr's hands as he pressed forward and after capturing Zarahemla he continued his march farther north, slaying great numbers of the Nephites as he advanced. Coriantumr having thus thrown himself into the centre of the Nephite country and failed to properly keep up the communications in his rear was at the mercy of Moronihah as soon as the later could gather in his troops. Moronihah first sent Lehi, with an army, to intercept the Lamanites' progress northward before they could reach the land Bountiful. In this Lehi was successful. He gave them battle and compelled them to retreat towards Zarahemla. Before they could reach there Moronihah met them. A bloody battle

they could not rely upon wood for their construction materials, these colonists resorted to working with cement, although they also imported wood from the south and attempted, as a long-term solution to the problem, to reforest the land. (Hel. 3:7-10.) Meanwhile, back in the central Nephite lands, the affairs of the people alternately prospered and decayed. In 43 B.C., for example, peace had been established and the church was flourishing. Referring to this time, Mormon noted that such blessings were-and always had been-open to all. "Thus," he wrote, "we may see that the Lord is merciful unto all who will, in the sincerity of their hearts, call upon his holy name. Yea, thus we see that the gate of heaven is open unto all, even to those who will believe on the name of Jesus Christ, who is the Son of God. Yea, we see that whosoever will may lay hold upon the word of God, which is quick and powerful, which shall divide asunder all the cunning and the snares and the wiles of the devil, and lead the man of Christ in a straight and narrow course across that everlasting gulf of misery which is prepared to engulf the wicked-and land their souls, yea, their immortal souls, at the right hand of God in the kingdom of heaven, to sit down with Abraham, and Isaac, and with Jacob, and with all our holy fathers, to go no more out." (Hel. 3:27-30.) There was never any need in Nephite history-and there is none in our own time-for the suffering and misery that inevitably accompany wickedness. Yet we are prey to the same temptations as our ancestors. One of the hopes of the scriptural writers who so frankly recorded the sins and errors of their own times was that we of a latter day might profit from their experiences, and, in many cases, avoid repeating them. (See, for example, Morm. 9:31 and Ps. 78:5-8.) How are we to avoid falling into the same snares as did many of those before us? In his entry for 41 B.C., Mormon gave us a large portion of the answer to that question. In that year, class distinctions began again to appear within the church, and some members of the church gave themselves over to pride and arrogance, and to mistreating the more humble among their brothers and sisters. But amidst all the pain and despite all the discomfort that must have attended this situation, these humble saints remained faithful to God and to their covenants. We are told that "they did fast and pray oft, and did wax stronger and stronger in their humility, and firmer and firmer in the faith of Christ, unto the filling their souls with joy and consolation, yea, even to the purifying and the sanctification of their hearts, which sanctification cometh because of their yielding their hearts unto God." (Hel. 3:35.) Thus, in the face of adversity and perhaps even pain, the Lord's true saints drew yet closer to him, through fasting, prayer, and humble obedience. His true saints do so today, as well. "Except the Lord Keep the City . . ." For the year 38 B.C., Mormon's abridgment refers briefly to the rise of contention within the church, which became so severe that it eventually resulted in violence and bloodshed. "And the rebellious part were slain and driven out of the land, and they did go unto the king of the Lamanites. And it came to pass that they did endeavor to stir up the Lamanites to war against the Nephites." (Hel. 4:2-3.) However, the Lamanites had been beaten too many times in the memory of many still living to be very enthusiastic about yet another assault on the Nephites; they were, in fact, "exceedingly afraid." (Hel. 4:3.) The great war narrated in the Book of Alma had ended in utter Lamanite defeat only a little more than two decades earlier, and the spectacular campaign led by Coriantumr had concluded disastrously just thirteen years before. And both of these military catastrophes had been set in motion by dissenters from the Nephites, who often seemed more than willing to fight their erstwhile brethren down to the last drop of Lamanite blood. Thus, the Lamanites did not respond favorably to the incitements of this particular group of dissenters. But yet another wave of apostates arrived in Lamanite territory in the following year, and, with them fanning the flames kindled by the earlier group, the resistance of the Lamanites began to weaken. Finally, in 35 B.C., the Lamanites again launched an invasion of Nephite territory. By the next year, they had taken Zarahemla, the Nephite capital. Moronihah and his armies attempted a counterattack, but by 31 B.C. they had managed to recover only half of their original possessions. It is at this point that Mormon, himself a great military commander, interrupted his narrative to offer some observations on the reasons behind the Nephites' inability to respond effectively to this invasion. It is highly interesting, however, that his remarks not only do not center on military strategy and tactical

But when their keepers came to fetch them for execution, the Lord intervened. "Nephi and Lehi were encircled about as if by fire, even insomuch that they durst not lay their hands upon them." (Hel. 5:23.) Encouraged by this, the brothers began to preach to their would-be killers-accompanied by earthquakes, a preternatural "cloud of darkness," and a voice from heaven. Quite evidently, the Lord was working here in a major way, for purposes important to him. And, indeed, his intervention in this instance would be of major historical importance, as succeeding chapters of Helaman make clear. In the face of this supernatural display, the Lamanites were immobilized. Fortunately, one of the apostate Nephites was nearby. Such apostates could be the most bitter and implacable of enemies to the truths of God, but they were also capable-as in the case of this man, one Aminadab-of dredging up from deep in their memories the childhood teachings that would allow them to receive the gospel and teach it to others. Aminadab was able to answer the now earnest inquiries of the Lamanites, and to explain to them how they could be delivered from the cloud of darkness that had engulfed them. Calling upon the Lord, the newly humbled Lamanites were delivered-and not only so, but they were visited by angels and commended by the heavenly voice. This powerful experience created an instant missionary force of three hundred eager converts. Within a short time, "the more part of the Lamanites were convinced of them." (Hel. 5:50.) The Tables Turned Nephi and Lehi had apparently had little success preaching among the Nephites, but they had triumphed spectacularly with the Lamanites and their sympathizers. There ensued now a period that explodes the oversimplified view of the Book of Mormon held by some who do not know it well. For them, the book is a long but uncomplicated tale of white-hatted Nephites and black-hatted Lamanite villains. Certainly, however, for the period under discussion here nothing could be further from the truth. Helaman 6 describes a time when the Lamanites were righteous, and the Nephites wicked. The Lamanites even sent missionaries among their former enemies and were able, at least temporarily, to bring many of them to the truth and to repentance. And their prosperity was not simply spiritual. With many Nephites and Lamanites converted to the Lord, peace was established, barriers to free trade fell, and-as a predictable consequence-the entire land became prosperous. (Hel. 6:7-9.) Wealth, however, carries with it major risks to the spiritual well-being of those who possess it. It can, in fact, come to possess them. Sometimes, oddly, those who have more than enough of worldly goods can become more obsessed with them than those who must struggle just to make ends meet. So it was with the Nephites at this time. "They began to set their hearts upon their riches." (Hel. 6:17.) They resurrected the old conspiratorial oaths and plans of Gadianton's band. The Lamanites were horrified at this new outbreak of Gadiantonism and made every effort to eradicate it. The Nephites, on the other hand, took to the new possibilities for organized evil with notable enthusiasm. By 24 B.C., their government was firmly in the hands of a revolutionary sect that had taken its immediate beginning in the political turmoil of a quarter of a century earlier. Chapters 1-6 of Helaman end, thus, on a rather depressing note. From the gospel of Christ, the Nephites had turned to a set of practices and doctrines originating with his archenemy. Of these, Mormon wrote, "They were put into the heart of Gadianton by that same being who did entice our first parents to partake of the forbidden fruit-yea, that same being who did plot with Cain, that if he would murder his brother Abel it should not be known unto the world. And he did plot with Cain and his followers from that time forth." (Hel. 6:26-27; cf. Moses 5:29-33, 47-51.) Nevertheless, Mormon did not leave us without hope. Under the inspiration of the Lord he gave us important keys for avoiding, individually and as a society, the fate of the Nephites, and he stressed to us that the Lord's blessing and help is available freely, and to all. Surely the counsel of Helaman to his sons, Nephi and Lehi, was preserved on the golden plates because of its continuing relevance to our day: "My sons," said Helaman, "remember, remember that it is upon the rock of our Redeemer, who is Christ, the Son of God, that ye must build your foundation; that when the devil shall send forth his mighty winds, yea, his shafts in the whirlwind, yea, when all his hail and his mighty storm shall beat upon you, it shall have no power over you to drag you down to the gulf of misery and endless wo, because of the rock upon which ye are built, which is a sure foundation, a foundation whereon if men build they cannot fall."

just thirteen years before. And both of these military catastrophes had been set in motion by dissenters from the Nephites, who often seemed more than willing to fight their erstwhile brethren down to the last drop of Lamanite blood. Thus, the Lamanites did not respond favorably to the incitements of this particular group of dissenters. But yet another wave of apostates arrived in Lamanite territory in the following year, and, with them fanning the flames kindled by the earlier group, the resistance of the Lamanites began to weaken. Finally, in 35 B.C., the Lamanites again launched an invasion of Nephite territory. By the next year, they had taken Zarahemla, the Nephite capital. Moronihah and his armies attempted a counterattack, but by 31 B.C. they had managed to recover only half of their original possessions. It is at this point that Mormon, himself a great military commander, interrupted his narrative to offer some observations on the reasons behind the Nephites' inability to respond effectively to this invasion. It is highly interesting, however, that his remarks not only do not center on military strategy and tactical considerations, but indeed do not raise such issues at all. Rather, he wrote, the cause of the Nephites' weakness was their wickedness-a wickedness that extended even into the church of God. "And it was because of the pride of their hearts, because of their exceeding riches, yea, it was because of their oppression to the poor, withholding their food from the hungry, withholding their clothing from the naked, and smiting their humble brethren upon the cheek, making a mock of that which was sacred, denying the spirit of prophecy and of revelation, murdering, plundering, lying, stealing, committing adultery, rising up in great contentions, and deserting away into the land of Nephi, among the Lamanites-and because of this their great wickedness, and their boastings in their own strength, they were left in their own strength." (Hel. 4:12-13, emphasis added.) Mormon, of course, was sufficiently astute as a military strategist to know what it would mean for the Nephites to be left to merely their own strength. They had been vastly outnumbered by the Lamanites for many generations. If it came to a struggle of numbers, there could be only one outcome. Even the contemporary Nephites, wicked as they were, could see the disastrous implications of their course: "And they saw that they had become weak, like unto their brethren, the Lamanites, and that the Spirit of the Lord did no more preserve them; and they saw that the Lamanites were exceedingly more numerous than they, and except they should cleave unto the Lord their God they must unavoidably perish. For behold, they saw that the strength of the Lamanites was as great as their strength, even man for man." (Hel. 4:24-26.) The greatness of Mormon, as well as of Moroni, consisted at least partially of the fact that, competent as the Nephites were, they continued to recognize that the Lord is the ultimate arbiter of human affairs. And they never became so obsessed with military tactics and technology as to forget that it is a nation's moral and spiritual resources that will most surely and fundamentally determine its fate. "Except the Lord keep the city," wrote the Psalmist, "the watchman waketh but in vain." (Ps. 127:1.) Roots Not Branches Nephi, son of Helaman, knew, fully as well as would the later chronicler Mormon, that the fundamental problem of the Nephites at this period was spiritual rather than military. For this reason, he decided to devote all his energies to a spiritual solution. In that same year, 30 B.C., he resigned the judgment seat and, with his brother Lehi, undertook full-time preaching of the gospel. The task was urgent if the Nephites were to be saved from themselves. "For as their laws and their governments were established by the voice of the people, and they who chose evil were more numerous than they who chose good, therefore they were ripening for destruction, for the laws had become corrupted." (Hel. 5:2.) This was precisely the situation King Mosiah II had foreseen as a frightening but remote possibility six decades earlier, when he abolished the monarchy and established the Nephite system of judges. The laws of the Nephites, he had said, were correct and divinely given. Further, he had said, placing the direction of the nation in the hands of a more representative form of government would tend to ensure the continuation of those laws in their correct form, since the majority of the people were unlikely to choose evil over good. But, he had continued, "if the time comes that the voice of the people doth choose iniquity, then is the time that the judgments of God will come upon you." (Mosiah 29:25-27.) And now, probably within the lifetimes of some who had heard him give that historic address,

operate frequently in the next several centuries-offering a textbook illustration of guerrilla warfare. They did not fade away. "More of this Gadianton shall be spoken hereafter," wrote Mormon. "And behold, in the end of this book [by which he meant the Book of Mormon as a whole] ye shall see that this Gadianton did prove the overthrow, yea, almost the entire destruction of the people of Nephi." (Hel. 2:12-13.) A Season of Emigration to the North Helaman 3 records that many people left the land of Zarahemla in 46 B.C. and made their way into the land to the north. (In this northward emigration, they were following the precedent that had been set a decade earlier by Hagoth and his people. See Alma 63:4-10.) They traveled a great distance to a region of "large bodies of water and many rivers." (Hel. 3:4.) This area had previously sustained a large population that the Nephites identified with the earlier people known as the Jaredites (see Ether 1:1), and it had (in a situation we today can well imagine) been stripped of its timber. Thus, given the fact that they could not rely upon wood for their construction materials, these colonists resorted to working with cement, although they also imported wood from the south and attempted, as a long-term solution to the problem, to reforest the land. (Hel. 3:7-10.) Meanwhile, back in the central Nephite lands, the affairs of the people alternately prospered and decayed. In 43 B.C., for example, peace had been established and the church was flourishing. Referring to this time, Mormon noted that such blessings were-and always had been-open to all. "Thus," he wrote, "we may see that the Lord is merciful unto all who will, in the sincerity of their hearts, call upon his holy name. Yea, thus we see that the gate of heaven is open unto all, even to those who will believe on the name of Jesus Christ, who is the Son of God. Yea, we see that whosoever will may lay hold upon the word of God, which is quick and powerful, which shall divide asunder all the cunning and the snares and the wiles of the devil, and lead the man of Christ in a straight and narrow course across that everlasting gulf of misery which is prepared to engulf the wicked-and land their souls, yea, their immortal souls, at the right hand of God in the kingdom of heaven, to sit down with Abraham, and Isaac, and with Jacob, and with all our holy fathers, to go no more out." (Hel. 3:27-30.) There was never any need in Nephite history-and there is none in our own time-for the suffering and misery that inevitably accompany wickedness. Yet we are prey to the same temptations as our ancestors. One of the hopes of the scriptural writers who so frankly recorded the sins and errors of their own times was that we of a latter day might profit from their experiences, and, in many cases, avoid repeating them. (See, for example, Morm. 9:31 and Ps. 78:5-8.) How are we to avoid falling into the same snares as did many of those before us? In his entry for 41 B.C., Mormon gave us a large portion of the answer to that question. In that year, class distinctions began again to appear within the church, and some members of the church gave themselves over to pride and arrogance, and to mistreating the more humble among their brothers and sisters. But amidst all the pain and despite all the discomfort that must have attended this situation, these humble saints remained faithful to God and to their covenants. We are told that "they did fast and pray oft, and did wax stronger and stronger in their humility, and firmer and firmer in the faith of Christ, unto the filling their souls with joy and consolation, yea, even to the purifying and the sanctification of their hearts, which sanctification cometh because of their yielding their hearts unto God." (Hel. 3:35.) Thus, in the face of adversity and perhaps even pain, the Lord's true saints drew yet closer to him, through fasting, prayer, and humble obedience. His true saints do so today, as well. "Except the Lord Keep the City . . ." For the year 38 B.C., Mormon's abridgment refers briefly to the rise of contention within the church, which became so severe that it eventually resulted in violence and bloodshed. "And the rebellious part were slain and driven out of the land, and they did go unto the king of the Lamanites. And it came to pass that they did endeavor to stir up the Lamanites to war against the Nephites." (Hel. 4:2-3.) However, the Lamanites had been beaten too many times in the memory of many still living to be very enthusiastic about yet another assault on the Nephites; they were, in fact, "exceedingly afraid." (Hel. 4:3.) The great war narrated in the Book of Alma had ended in utter Lamanite defeat only a little more than two decades earlier, and the spectacular campaign led by Coriantumr had concluded disastrously

quotes.text: Mexico. See Sorenson, Ancient American Setting, pp. 266-67. It would seem, at any event, that the colonization of Teotihuacán occurred during the same period as the Nephite-Lamanite migrations to the north that are recorded in this and adjacent portions of the Book of Mormon. See David A. Palmer, In Search of Cumorah (Bountiful: Horizon Publishers, 1981), pp. 188-91. A variant of the Sorenson model (Palmer, Cumorah, p. 186) suggests the area of modern Veracruz as a possible site. 11. In conversation with Prof. John L. Sorenson on this point, he suggests that if the area is, in fact, to be identified with the Valley of Mexico, it is probably too far north to have actually been part of historic Jaredite territory. However, Nephite knowledge of northern geography beyond a certain point seems to have been vague at best. Certainly they thought this area to have been in formerly Jaredite country. 12. Mormon was to face the same mindset in his own day, and with even more calamitous results. See Mormon 4:8; 5:2, 6. 13. A challenging modern counterpart to this ancient principle may be found in the notable essay by President Spencer W. Kimball, entitled ''The False Gods We Worship,'' in the Ensign for June 1976. 14. Nephi's spiritual sensitivity, with that of his brother, Lehi, probably owed much to the teaching of their father, Helaman. Hel. 5:6-7 speaks volumes of his desire to instill in them a sense of heritage and of the models they were to emulate. See also Hel. 5:8-12. 15. Mosiah's comments had been recalled a decade later by Amulek, speaking to the people of Ammonihah. (See Alma 10:19.) The similarity between the wording of this passage and that of Helaman 5:2 is striking. 16. For much of the remainder of the Book of Helaman, it is the Lamanites and not the Nephites who were the righteous followers of the Lord. This was, of course, the period of Samuel the Lamanite. 17. One recalls also the dissenters at Zarahemla. (See above, and Hel. 5:17.) This thought can be of comfort to parents who have watched their children reject their teachings and drift from or rebel against the Church. Gospel seeds once planted can bear fruit even many years later. 18. The principle underlying this situation is put in modern terms by the Nobel laureate economist Paul A. Samuelson, in his famous textbook, Economics, 8th ed. (New York: Mc Graw-Hill, 1970), p. 668: ''There is essentially only one argument for free or freer trade, but it is an exceedingly powerful one, namely: Unhampered trade promotes a mutually profitable international division of labor, greatly enhances the potential real national product of all countries, and makes possible higher standards of living all over the globe.'' (Emphasis in the original.) That the Book of Mormon so well depicts the operation of an economic law that was not generally recognized in Joseph Smith's time-and perhaps is not yet, even today-is a good illustration of its plausibility as authentic historiographical material.
quotes.attr: Daniel C. Peterson in Kent P. Jackson, ed., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 8: Alma 30 to Moroni, p.92-103

quotes.text: Their Own Worst Enemies (Helaman 1-6) Daniel C. Peterson Chapters 1-6 of Helaman cover fully thirty years and offer us a fairly good glimpse into how Mormon the editor worked. This section of the Book of Mormon is largely an example of what is often termed "annalistic" writing. "Annals," a type of historical record-keeping with many Near Eastern parallels, feature a narrative of events listed year by year. This is precisely what we find in these chapters. With only a single exception, every one of the thirty years extending from the fortieth year of the judges up to the sixty-ninth (from 52 to 23 B.C.) is explicitly mentioned in these chapters. Sometimes, it is true, the year is cited very briefly, with only the most perfunctory indication of the state of the Nephites or of some event that occurred. Consider, for example, Helaman 3:1-2: In these verses, Mormon tells us very briefly that 49 B.C. was characterized by a small problem with pride which was settled in that same year, and that the years 48 and 47 saw little or no contention. Likewise, Helaman 3:32-33 chronicles very briefly the years 43-40 B.C., which saw the Nephites decline from "peace and exceedingly great joy" to a cancerously growing pride, and Helaman 4:1-10 carries us from 38 to 31 B.C. We know already from the title page of the Book of Mormon that it is "an abridgment of the record of the people of Nephi," and we know that it was Mormon himself who was responsible for most of the condensation. It is possible, almost, to picture him at his task: He sits at a sort of writing table, surrounded by centuries of the records of his people. (Perhaps, given his central role in the violent events of his time, he does most of his writing in stolen moments in a military commander's field tent.) As he reads through the chronicles that have come to him, he knows he can include only a small portion in his abridgment. Therefore, under the inspiration of the Lord, he chooses what he thinks important and interesting, making occasional comments on the material he selects. However, he is too honest, too conscientious and thorough, to simply leave whole periods out of the record. Thus, for example, when he comes to his sources for the years 43-40 B.C., he finds a host of events and details that he cannot put into his book. But he can draw a lesson from them, and in this case the moral he distills from his source-chronicles is the evil of pride. Rarely did Mormon go into an event or a sermon in great detail. Much of the record briefly summarizes what must have been a vast archive of source materials.
quotes.speaker: This is
quotes.attr: This is, of course, one of the frustrations of those who would read the Book of Mormon as an archaeological guidebook or as a geographical manual-such historical, archaeological, and geographical details as exist in it are there incidentally and sketchily. Mormon was serious about his assignment

quotes.text: The book of Helaman treats four themes. It begins with contention over government when three of Pahoran's sons want to be the chief judge. They hold an election, and the voice of the people elects one of the three. One of those defeated accepts the voice of the people, but the other rebels and tries to set himself up as the king. This contention over control of the government led to the organization of secret combinations, the second theme of the book of Helaman. These first two themes are found in the first six chapters. The third theme, found in Hel. 7-12, tells about the drought that was caused when Nephi sealed the heavens to humble his people that they might return to God. The fourth theme is found in chapters 13-16, which tell how Samuel the Lamanite stood on the walls of Zarahemla and prophesied of the birth, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. The whole book of Helaman covers approximately fifty years, from 52 to 1 B.C.
quotes.speaker: Monte S. Nyman
quotes.source: The Most Correct Book [Salt Lake City: Bookcraft
quotes.attr: Monte S. Nyman, The Most Correct Book [Salt Lake City: Bookcraft, 1991], 42 43

quotes.text: Two spectacular events dominate the first chapter of the book of Helaman—a succession crisis accompanied by an assassination, and a successful but short-lived Lamanite seizure of the central Nephite lands. However, neither of these events proved as significant in the long term as the rise of a terrorist group that came to be called the "Gadianton robbers.
quotes.attr: Daniel C. Peterson in Kent P. Jackson, ed., Studies in Scripture, Vol. 8: Alma 30 to Moroni [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1988], 93
